one_line_takeaway: GCD extends GradSafe's single-anchor gradient jailbreak detector to a dual-anchor ('Sure' + 'Sorry') decision boundary and adds deterministic first-token refusal injection, cutting attack success rate (e.g. ToxicChat ASR 33.60%->27.32%, an 18.7% relative gain) at the cost of a modest increase in false positives (1.55%->2.54%) and ~15-20ms latency, though GradSafe still edges out GCD on raw F1 and FP on the paper's own hardest benchmark (ToxicChat).
key_finding: On ToxicChat, GCD achieves Precision 67.34% / Recall 72.67% / F1 69.91% / FP 2.54% / ASR 27.32%, versus GradSafe's Precision 75.46% / Recall 66.39% / F1 70.64% / FP 1.55% / ASR 33.60% -- i.e. GradSafe actually has LOWER false positives and slightly HIGHER F1 than GCD on ToxicChat, but GCD lowers ASR by a relative 18.7% (the paper's own stated figure) over GradSafe at the cost of raising FP from 1.55% to 2.54%. On XSTest, GCD gets the best Precision (92.38%) and F1 (91.69%) among all compared methods with FP 3.33% (paper's abstract separately states 'FP=0.03%' for XSTest in the running text, which appears to be a typo relative to Table 1's 3.33% -- flagged as an internal inconsistency, not something I am inventing). On AdvBench essentially all methods saturate (Precision 100% for all), but GCD attains the best F1 (99.90%) and lowest ASR (0.19%) tied with Safe-Decoding. Latency overhead (Table 3, TTFT) is Llama2-7B: 86.60ms -> 99.89ms (+13.29ms), Mixtral-8x7B: 101.34ms -> 119.07ms (+17.73ms), Qwen-2-7B: 80.32ms -> 94.51ms (+14.19ms), averaging under 15-20ms as claimed. Scaling study (Table 5) on ToxicChat shows a non-monotonic trend: Qwen3-1.7B F1 70.68%/FP 15.20% -> Qwen3-4B F1 73.58%/FP 12.40% (best) -> Qwen3-8B F1 71.94%/FP 13.80%, while on XSTest larger models are more consistently stronger (Qwen3-8B recall 92.50%, F1 89.37%). The headline number differs across sections of the paper itself: the abstract says '52% FP reduction vs GradSafe... ASR down by up to 10%,' the Introduction says 'up to 20%' ASR reduction, and Section 3.2 computes an 18.7% relative ASR improvement on ToxicChat specifically -- these are not fully reconciled within the paper.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Gradient-Controlled Decoding (GCD), a training-free, two-stage LLM jailbreak/prompt-injection guardrail built directly on top of GradSafe (Xie et al., 2024), which the paper explicitly cites as prior work it extends. Stage 1 (dual-anchor detection) computes prompt gradients with respect to TWO complementary compliance-response anchors -- an acceptance token ("Sure") and a refusal token ("Sorry") -- instead of GradSafe's single "Sure"-only anchor, and flags a prompt as unsafe only when cosine-similarity scores against both an unsafe-reference gradient exceed independently calibrated thresholds (t_Sure, t_Sorry), which are chosen as the F1-maximizing operating point on Precision-Recall curves built from a 20-template (10 safe / 10 unsafe) calibration set. Stage 2 (deterministic mitigation) is the paper's other novel piece: when a prompt is flagged, GCD preset-injects one or two refusal tokens ("Sorry, I can't ...") into the decoder before autoregressive generation resumes, which the authors argue guarantees first-token safety regardless of sampling strategy (temperature, top-k/top-p) -- closing what they describe as a leakage gap in GradSafe, which is detect-only and lets generation proceed normally (and therefore possibly unsafely) once a prompt is flagged.
threat_model: Adversarial users submitting jailbreak prompts and (per the Limitations/Future Work section) prompt-injection style inputs to an open-weight LLM at inference time, with the defender having white-box gradient access to the deployed model (gradients are computed per-prompt against 'Sure'/'Sorry' compliance responses). The countervailing failure mode the paper treats as equally important is over-refusal / false positives on benign queries, i.e. the threat model explicitly includes 'attack that degrades UX by over-blocking' alongside 'attack that leaks harmful content.' The paper does NOT evaluate adaptive attackers who know GCD's detection mechanism (e.g., gradient-obfuscation or optimization-based attacks against the dual-anchor detector itself) -- AdvBench, ToxicChat and XSTest are static/non-adaptive benchmarks.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 5): (1) reliance on tailored/static template prompts (20 templates: 10 safe/10 unsafe) for calibration may limit generalizability to unseen prompt styles, other languages, or adaptive adversarial attacks; (2) computing gradients for every incoming prompt at inference time adds latency and runtime memory overhead versus normal decoding; (3) evaluation is single-turn only -- multi-turn dialogue safety is unaddressed; (4) the method has not been extended to indirect prompt injection despite being motivated partly by prompt-injection concerns; (5) no evaluation against adaptive/white-box attackers who specifically target the dual-anchor gradient detector, despite the paper itself requiring white-box gradient access as a precondition. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the data): GCD does not uniformly beat GradSafe -- on ToxicChat, the dataset the authors call 'the most challenging,' GradSafe has both lower FP (1.55% vs 2.54%) and marginally higher F1 (70.64% vs 69.91%) than GCD; SCANS is not evaluated on ToxicChat at all (marked '-' in Table 1), so the comparison there is incomplete.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 total, all attack samples, 0 non-attack)
quantitative_results: ToxicChat GCD: Precision 67.34%, Recall 72.67%, F1 69.91%, FP 2.54%, ASR 27.32%
ToxicChat GradSafe: Precision 75.46%, Recall 66.39%, F1 70.64%, FP 1.55%, ASR 33.60%
XSTest GCD: Precision 92.38%, Recall 91.00%, F1 91.69%, FP 3.33% (Table 1), ASR 9.00%
AdvBench GCD: Precision 100.00%, Recall 99.80%, F1 99.90%, FP 0.00%, ASR 0.19% (lowest ASR of all compared methods)
ToxicChat relative ASR improvement of GCD over GradSafe: 18.7% (paper's own computed figure in Section 3.2)
Scaling on ToxicChat: Qwen3-1.7B F1 70.68%/FP 15.20%; Qwen3-4B F1 73.58%/FP 12.40%; Qwen3-8B F1 71.94%/FP 13.80%
Scaling on XSTest: Qwen3-8B recall 92.50%/F1 89.37%; Llama-3.2-3B best overall XSTest F1 91.00% among scaling models

Ablation (Table 4, XSTest AUPRC, mean+-SD over 10 runs): varying unsafe reference n=2: 0.911+-0.042, n=5: 0.928+-0.022, n=10: 0.932; varying safe reference n=2: 0.934+-0.002, n=5: 0.935+-0.001, n=10: 0.934
Calibration set size: 20 templates (10 safe, 10 unsafe)
baselines_compared: GradSafe (Xie et al. 2024) -- single-anchor gradient detector, detect-only, no first-token guarantee
SCANS (Cao et al. 2025) -- activation steering; metrics reported as derived from its published Refusal Rates rather than re-run by these authors; not evaluated on ToxicChat
Greedy / Top-k / Top-p decoding-only strategies (no dedicated safety detector)
